revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Akita Kantō]][[fa:آکیتا کانتو]][[fr:Akita kantō matsuri]][[id:Akita Kantō]][[ja:竿燈]][[ko:아키타 간토 축제]][[or:ଅକିତା କାନ୍ତୋ]][[zh:竿燈]]
{{Use dmy dates|date=September 2021|qq=}} {{Infobox holiday |type = shinto |image = Akita Kanto.jpg |imagesize = 250px |caption = Celebrant of the Akita Kantō |official_name = |nickname = |observedby = [[:en:Akita, Akita|Akita City]] |litcolor = |longtype = Religious |significance = |begins = 3 August |ends = 7 August |date = 3 August |scheduling = same day each year |duration = 5 days |celebrations = |observances = |relatedto = {{ill|Fukagawa Matsuri|en|Fukagawa Matsuri|qid=Q3090688|lt=Fukagawa Matsuri|1=Fukagawa Matsuri}}, {{ill|Sannō Matsuri|en|Sannō Matsuri|qid=Q218663|lt=Sannō Matsuri|1=Sannō Matsuri}} |frequency = annual }} [[File:Akita Kanto Festival 2017.jpg|thumb|260px|Kanto Festival at night (August 2017)]] The {{nihongo|'''Akita Kantō'''|秋田竿燈まつり|qq=}} is a [[:en:Japanese festival|Japanese festival]] celebrated from 3–7 August in [[:en:Akita, Akita|Akita City]], {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}} in hope for a good harvest. Around two hundred {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|qid=Q670887|lt=Bamboo|1=Bamboo}} poles five to twelve metres long, bearing twenty-four or forty-six [[:en:Japanese lantern|lanterns]], topped with ''[[gohei]]'', and weighing up to fifty kilograms, are carried through the streets by night on the palms, foreheads, shoulders, or lower backs of the celebrants.<ref name="ex02">{{cite web |url=http://www.kantou.gr.jp/english/index.htm |title=Akita Kantō official website|publisher=Akita Kantō Executive Committee |qq=}}</ref> The festival is first referred to in a{{who|date=October 2014|qq=}} travel diary of 1789 {{nihongo|''The Road Where the Snow Falls''|雪の降る道|qq=}}.<ref name="ex02"/> It is one of the main festivals in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]], along with the {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|qid=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}} festival in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|qid=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}, the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|qid=Q862407|lt=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}} festival, and the {{ill|Yamagata Hanagasa Festival|en|Yamagata Hanagasa Festival|qid=Q11615626|lt=Yamagata Hanagasa Festival|1=Yamagata Hanagasa Festival}} in [[:en:Yamagata, Yamagata|Yamagata]]. The Akita Kantō festival was designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan|en|Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan|qid=Q11644860|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan}} in 1980.<ref name="ex03">{{cite web|url=http://www.city.akita.akita.jp/en/sightseeing/matsuri/default.htm|title=Akita Kantō festival|publisher=Akita City|access-date=22 November 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150916050423/http://www.city.akita.akita.jp/en/sightseeing/matsuri/default.htm|archive-date=16 September 2015|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a42_fes_akita.html |title=Akita Kanto Matsuri |publisher=[[Japan National Tourism Organization]] |accessdate=20 March 2011| archiveurl= https://web.archive.org/web/20110218065322/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a42_fes_akita.html| archivedate= 18 February 2011 | url-status= live|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.pref.akita.jp/e/0501.html |title=Seasonal Festivals (Matsuri) |publisher={{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}} |accessdate=20 March 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110911180821/http://www.pref.akita.jp/e/0501.html |archivedate=11 September 2011 |qq=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191223161201/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/18 |url-status=dead |archive-date=23 December 2019 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 March 2011 |qq=}}</ref> == History == This festival originated from [[Neburi Nagashi]] which was held for ridding illness and maliciousness in summer. It already existed in the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Horeki Period|1=Hōreki}} (1751–1764), in the middle of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo era|1=Edo period}}. "Yuki no huru michi (The road where it snows)" written by [[Soan Tsumura]] in 1789 is regarded as the oldest document which described Neburi Nagashi. It describes that Neburi Nagashi was held on 6 July of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] and introduced as the original tradition of Akita. Also, Neburi Nagashi was an annual event to pray for good harvests and artistic progress. In the Neburi Nagashi around [[:en:Akita, Akita|Akita city]], people decorated silk trees and bamboo grasses with strips of paper on which they had written their wishes. Then, they walked around the city with them and floated them downstream. In Neburi Nagashi, people combined candles and lanterns. This instrument for Neburi Nagashi became called Kanto. The present official name of the event was first used by [[Tetsusaku Okubo]] in 1881, when he suggested the idea of entertaining the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Meiji Emperor|1=Emperor Meiji}} with the Kanto performance to those who were in charge of hosting the Emperor during his visit to Akita.<ref name="ex01">Sayaka Hashimoto. "Artistic Weight Lifting KANTOU: Tradition and Acculturation”. https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ijshs/4/Special_Issue_2006/4_Special_Issue_2006_187/_article.</ref> Since the lunar calendar changed to the solar calendar in 1872, the Kanto festival was compelled to be held one month earlier.<ref name="ex01"/> However, the number of Kanto, which had been 50 in 1900, had dramatically decreased due to changes of festival’s schedule and its site. The numbers of Kanto which participated in the Kanto festival in 1905 were only 4 or 5 and this situation made the future of the festival uncertain. {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|qid=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho|1=Emperor Taishō}} visited Akita and appreciated Kanto performance in 1908. Also a soft drink factory started to advertise its beverages on Kanto’s lanterns in 1909. These two events helped the restoration of Kanto festival. Then, the festival’s schedule changed to the lunar calendar again to avoid the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|qid=Q658486|lt=rainy season|1=East Asian rainy season}} and the number of visitors increased. In 1931, the Kanto Society, which managed the Kanto Skill Festival (Myogikai), was founded. Although Kanto festival was canceled between 1938 and 1946 due to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=the Second World War|1=Pacific War}}, it resumed after the war. The Kanto Festival Executive Committee was established in 1966. While the Kanto Society had been managing the Kanto Skill Festival, the Kanto Festival Executive Committee was in charge of the operation of the Kanto Festival. Triggered by the First Kanto Performance overseas in {{ill|San Diego|en|San Diego|qid=Q16552|lt=San Diego|1=San Diego}}, US, in 1976, the Kanto began to be performed in various countries.<ref name="ex01"/> The date of the festival had changed three times. At present, the Kanto festival is held from 3 to 6 August every year. == Features == Kantō literally means "a pole with lanterns" and is made from bamboo poles and rice paper lanterns, which hang from horizontal bars.{{cn|date=August 2023|qq=}} When Kantō was invented, the lantern was hung in the garden. To convert it into a portable lantern, the dwarf bamboo that had been used for the lantern legs was replaced by longer bamboo.<ref name="ex01"/> The main bamboo pole of Kantō is called "Oyatake". All bamboo used for the main poles of Kantō are produced in Japan and are quite thick. Also, the regulations on thickness and the space between joints of the root are very strict.<ref name="ex03"/> Thus people who pick bamboos must select suitable bamboos to produce Kantō. The combined bamboos with main bamboo are called "Yokotake". A number of lanterns are suspended from each Yokotake. The bamboo added to lengthen Oyatake to extend the height of Kanto during a performance is called "Tsugidake". Kantō is classified into four categories; Oowaka, Chuwaka, Kowaka and Youwaka. The size, height and weight are regulated. (Table1) {| class="wikitable" |+ Table1 categories of Kanto and sizes<ref name="ex02"/> ! !! Length (m) !! Weight (kg) !! Size of lantern (cm) !! The number of lanterns |- ! Oowaka | 12 || 50 || 64*45 || 46 |- ! Chuwaka | 9 || 30 || 48*36 || 46 |- ! Kowaka | 7 || 15 || 48*36 || 24 |- ! Youwaka | 5 || 5 || 30*21 || 24 |} == Skills and contests == The techniques of Kanto are collectively called "Myogi". There are 5 categories of Myogi; Nagashi, Hirate (hand), Koshi (hip), Kata (shoulder) and Hitai (forehead). Performing groups, each consisting of 5 members, show these 5 techniques, one at time.<ref name="ex01"/> *'''Nagashi''' Performers support Kanto for other performers to add Tsugitake. They keep it on their palms and balance with their fingers. *'''Hirate (hand)''' Performers hold Kanto up higher. Then they add Tsugitake and keep it on their palms. *'''Koshi (hip)''' Performers hold Kanto with their fingers. Then they move it on their palms onto their hip. They bend the upper part of the body toward the side and balance with their legs. *'''Kata (shoulder)''' Performers keep Kanto on their palms of the dominant hand and make a straight line from the pivot leg to Kanto. Then they lift it higher. *'''Hitai (forehead)''' Performers hold Kanto with their fingers. Then they move it to their palms and put it on their foreheads. They keep this posture with their arms spread. The contest to compete with techniques of Kanto is called Myogikai or the daytime Kanto. The aim of Myogikai is "improving the skill of the Kanto as a whole by showing the Society members' skills to public and studying each other's skill."<ref name="ex01"/> Following this aim, performers maintain the unification and systemization of Kanto festival by upgrading their skills. Myogikai consists of team and individual competitions and performers who clear the preliminary can reach the final. Although both of them are judged in different ways, they compete in accuracy of skills, beauty of the posture and stability of Kanto. Since 1989, the skills competition has been complemented by musical performance. The rules for performance are strict. Time constraints are imposed for each skill and performers must perform in a circle which has a diameter of 6 meters. After all competitions, the winner in each competition is awarded a prize by Kanto Executive Committee. The night performance is a main event of Kanto festival. It is held in Kanto Oodori (one of the main streets in Akita city). During the night performance, performers don't compete against each other in skills and entertain visitors by showing their skills and illuminated Kanto. More than 230 Kanto are raised at the same time of the sound of {{ill|Shinobue|en|Shinobue|qid=Q3100072|lt=flutes|1=Shinobue}}.<ref name="ex02"/> ==See also== *[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]] *{{ill|Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan|en|Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan|qid=Q11644860|lt=Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan|1=Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Japan}} *{{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qid=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} ==External links== * [http://cgi2.nhk.or.jp/michi/cgi/detail.cgi?dasID=D0004200001_00000 Akita Kanto Matsuri]{{ill|NHK|en|NHK|qid=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}} {{coord|39|43|6.5|N|140|6|47.5|E|source:jawiki_type:event_region:JP-05_scale:5000|display=title|qq=}}
[[en:Akita Kantō]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Akita Kantō Festival}} [[fa:آکیتا کانتو]] [[fr:Akita kantō matsuri]] [[id:Akita Kantō]] [[ja:竿燈]] [[ko:아키타 간토 축제]] [[or:ଅକିତା କାନ୍ତୋ]] [[zh:竿燈]] {{wikidata link|Q4701224}} [[Category:提灯]] [[Category:Akita (city)]] [[Category:秋田県にある国指定の重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:Festivals in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:除災]]
